title: GDPR on Cardano
description: How GDPR maps to the framework — the bright line, breach notifications, consent lifecycle, and why the blockchain tensions don't apply.

stops.title: The Reframing
stops.narrative:
The natural question is 'can we put personal data on a blockchain?' The answer is no, and it is the wrong question.

[GDPR](node:gdpr) on Cardano is not about storing personal data on-chain. It is about storing **compliance evidence** on-chain: consent hashes, breach notification timestamps, rights-request response proofs. The chain proves the controller did what GDPR requires, when GDPR requires it. This is pure [process mode](node:process-mode).

stops.title: The Bright Line
stops.narrative:
The [bright line](node:bright-line) is the architectural principle that makes everything work: personal data off-chain, compliance evidence on-chain. No names, identifiers, health data, location, or behavioural profiles ever touch the chain — not even encrypted.

The chain stores hashes of compliance records. Under [Recital 26](https://gdpr-info.eu/recitals/no-26/)'s 'means reasonably likely' test, properly segregated hashes are not personal data. This resolves [erasure](node:tension-erasure), [controller designation](node:tension-controller-designation), and [data minimisation](node:tension-minimisation) tensions.

stops.title: The Schema Mapping
stops.narrative:
GDPR parties map cleanly to the four-party schema. The [supervisory authority](node:gdpr-supervisory-authority) maps to the [regulator](node:regulator) — maintains the regulation trie with controller standing. The [data controller](node:gdpr-controller) maps to the [operator](node:operator) — manages their compliance trie.

The [data subject](node:gdpr-data-subject) maps to the [user](node:user) — exercises rights via signing function. The [data processor](node:gdpr-processor) is a secondary operator with cross-referenced Art 28 agreements.

stops.title: 72-Hour Breach Notification
stops.narrative:
The flagship on-chain use case. [Art 33](https://gdpr-info.eu/art-33-gdpr/) requires notifying the SA within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach. Today, controllers self-report timing — there is no neutral witness.

The [commitment-then-submit](node:commitment-protocol) protocol turns part of this into a verifiable fact. The controller creates a commitment when it records the breach on-chain, and notification clears it. The chain proves how many slots elapsed between those two on-chain events. [Temporal integrity](node:temporal-integrity) replaces some self-reported claims, but not the need to assess off-chain awareness.

stops.title: Data Subject Rights (1 Month)
stops.narrative:
[Data subject rights](node:gdpr-rights-requests) — access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection — must be responded to within one month ([Art 12](https://gdpr-info.eu/art-12-gdpr/)), extendable to three months for complex requests.

Same pattern as breach notification: the [commitment protocol](node:commitment-protocol) creates a slot-bounded window. Timeout without submission is provable non-compliance. The [data subject](node:gdpr-data-subject) never needs a wallet — they interact through the controller's portal.

stops.title: Consent Lifecycle
stops.narrative:
[Consent](node:gdpr-consent) is modelled as a [lifecycle state machine](node:lifecycle-state-machine): None to Given to Withdrawn. Each transition is a leaf update with a timestamped hash.

The chain proves when consent was given, for what purpose (hash of description), when it was withdrawn, and that processing stopped after withdrawal. The [data controller](node:gdpr-controller) must demonstrate consent existed ([Art 7(1)](https://gdpr-info.eu/art-7-gdpr/)). The on-chain record is that demonstration.

stops.title: Certification as a Token
stops.narrative:
[GDPR certification](node:gdpr-certification) ([Art 42](https://gdpr-info.eu/art-42-gdpr/)) is voluntary, 3-year validity, issued by accredited bodies. On-chain, it maps naturally to a token — like a [beacon](node:beacon) but with a longer validity window.

The certification body is an authorised party in the [regulation trie](node:regulation-trie). The token carries an expiry slot. Expired or revoked tokens cannot be referenced in compliance submissions. Adherence to certification demonstrates compliance.

stops.title: Why the Tensions Don't Apply
stops.narrative:
The literature identifies fundamental conflicts between GDPR and blockchain. Every one of them concerns storing personal data on-chain.

[Immutability vs erasure](node:tension-erasure): no personal data to erase — compliance records prove accountability. [Controller designation](node:tension-controller-designation): clear — the controller operates their own trie. [Data minimisation](node:tension-minimisation): only hashes on-chain, minimal by design.

The [bright line](node:bright-line) resolves all three. [CNIL's 2018 guidance](https://www.cnil.fr/en/blockchain-and-gdpr-solutions-responsible-use-blockchain-context-personal-data) supports this approach.

stops.title: The Economics
stops.narrative:
[GDPR fines](node:gdpr-fines) provide the strongest [liveness](node:constraint-liveness) incentive of any regulation analysed — up to 4% of global turnover. A medium-sized controller would spend ~50-100 EUR/year in on-chain fees. A single fine (median ~50,000 EUR for SMEs, millions for large companies) dwarfs lifetime costs.

The value proposition is not cost savings — it is **proof**. The controller can prove to any court, SA, or data subject exactly when every compliance action occurred, with a neutral timestamp. This also strongly satisfies [fee alignment](node:constraint-fee-alignment).
